questions.prompt: Why is module-scope caching effective in Workers?

choices.text: Workers run inside isolates that handle many requests over their lifetime. Variables defined at module scope (outside the fetch handler) persist across all requests served by that isolate — so expensive one-time work (parsing a large config blob, compiling a regex, building a lookup table) runs once and the result is reused for free on subsequent requests. The catch: you have many isolates globally, so each region's first request pays the cost, and isolates can be evicted. Best for immutable or rarely-changing data; not a substitute for shared state (use KV / DO for that). Practical impact on CPU billing is substantial.

questions.explanation: Module-scope vars persist across requests in the same isolate — expensive init runs once, reused free.

questions.prompt: Worker costs $50/month for compute; upstream API calls cost $4000/month. Optimization priority?

choices.text: Cache the upstream — the Worker is essentially free; the API costs 80× more. Use the Cache API (or KV with TTL) to serve repeat requests from the edge without hitting the upstream. Even a 50% cache hit rate halves the $4000 bill to $2000 — Worker compute savings can never approach that. The general rule: optimize the most expensive line item, not the most visible one. Edge caching is exactly what Workers is best at; this is a textbook case for it. Worker CPU optimization is worth doing later, after the upstream is contained.

questions.explanation: Cache the expensive upstream. Worker compute is small change; the API bill is the leak.
